figures.caption: Fig. 1. Docking point shift in conventional two-stage mobile manipulation causes severe viewpoint variation, presents challenges for fixed-base manipulation. Our DockAnywhere utilizes trajectory-based viewpoint augmentation to generate diverse viewpoints from a single trajectory, ensuring that the manipulation policy can generalize to novel viewpoints caused by navigation errors.

Fig. 5. Real-world experiment visualization for task of placing gear onto plate. Source demonstration (left) in real world experiment of navigating and placing gear. Trained policy still works after transition (middle) and rotation (right) to simulate suboptimal docking points.

Fig. 2. Pipeline of the DockAnywhere framework. Given one source demonstration, DockAnywhere captures the point cloud and parses the trajectory based on the segmentation results. TAMP-based spatial transformation is applied to generate actions for relocated robot. Then the visual observations are synthesized by perceiving the robot’s end-effector and objects as 3D point clouds and relocating the position of the robot via point-level editing.

Fig. 4. Illustration of five simulated mobile manipulation tasks in ManiSkill environment with increasing difficulty: (1) Pick-up tasks. The robot navigates to a docking point and grasps the target object. (2) Pick-and-place tasks. The robot grasps the target object and place it into a designated container. (3) Complex-interaction tasks. The robot is required to interact with the handle to open the cabinet door or drawer without any collisions.
TABLE I MANISKILL SUCCESS RATES (SR, %) AND OVERALL SCORES ON ONE AND FIVE DOCKING POINTS. OVERALL SR IS THE AVERAGE OF ALL TASKS
